text: The robotic third arm lifts the divided inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) pedicle in a twelve o'clock position, separating the thin mesentery from the retroperitoneum. The pancreas is visible, and the surgeon pushes it down towards the retroperitoneum, elevating the descending mesocolon anteriorly. The splenic vein is visible in the thin mesocolon. The surgeon continues to push down the pancreas and retroperitoneal attachments, preparing to divide the lateral attachments of the splenic flexure.

The surgeon enters the lesser sac and proceeds laterally, pulling the splenic flexure down to expose the areolar tissue plane. The frontal colic ligament is visible as it is dissected, and the retroperitoneum is freed up. The omental colic attachment is peeled up, mobilizing the transverse colon. The medial mesentery is dissected to the border of the middle colic artery, ensuring no mesentery remains to the left or distal to the middle colic artery. The splenic flexure is fully mobilized using cautery in a medial to lateral approach.

The surgeon begins by incising the white line of Toldt, reflecting the descending colon towards the patient's right lower quadrant. The spleen and omentum are visible as the surgeon progresses towards the head. The omentocolic attachment is incised to enter the lesser sac, dividing the omentocolic, splenocolic, lenocolic, and phrenocolic ligaments to complete the lateral-to-medial dissection. The transverse colon is mobilized using the third arm of the robotic system, with the stomach visible to the left of the screen.
